perturbation.original_text: Seller will keep confidential and will not for a period of three (3) years after the execution of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, divulge to anyone, use or otherwise appropriate any of the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose whatsoever except to authorized representatives of Purchasers or the Parent.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller will keep confidential and will not for a period of ten (10) years after the execution of this Agreement, directly or indirectly, divulge to anyone, use or otherwise appropriate any of the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose whatsoever except to authorized representatives of Purchasers or the Parent.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the confidentiality period to 10 years may be deemed unreasonably long and unenforceable under New York law, especially if it lacks a specific business justification and harms the seller's ability to earn a living.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding the reasonableness of non-compete and confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: Eastern Artificial Insemination Co-op., Inc. v. LaBare, 210 A.D.2d 609, 619 N.Y.S.2d 858 (3d Dep’t 1994)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York courts scrutinize the reasonableness of non-compete and confidentiality agreements. While the contract is not directly non-compete, an unreasonably long confidentiality period (10 years) related to a sale can be deemed an unlawful restraint of trade if it is broader than necessary to protect the purchaser's legitimate interests, hurts the seller’s capacity to earn a livelihood, or injures the public. The courts will examine whether the time is longer than needed to protect the buyer. This may be seen as unreasonable without a specific protection need.
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confidentiality period from three to ten years, potentially contradicting legal standards for reasonable non-compete agreements under New York law. The Law Explanation suggests that excessively long confidentiality periods, especially those that hinder the seller's ability to earn a livelihood or are broader than necessary to protect the purchaser's legitimate interests, could be deemed unlawful restraints of trade. While there are no Scraped Law Snippets, the Law Explanation suggests the time must be justified. The extension of the confidentiality period to ten years, as described in the Perturbation Explanation, creates a strong indication of a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Seller agrees that for a period of three (3) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller agrees that for a period of five (5) years following the execution of this Agreement, it will not, without Purchasers' prior written consent, Directly or Indirectly Compete with Purchasers in the Business within the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period to five years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under New York law, particularly if it lacks a reasonable geographic scope or business justification.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding the reasonableness of non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: BDO Seidman v. Hirshberg, 93 N.Y.2d 382, 690 N.Y.S.2d 854 (1999)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in time and geographic scope and necessary to protect a legitimate business interest of the employer/purchaser. While the original three-year period may be defensible, a five-year period, particularly without a more narrowly defined geographic area or a showing of specific harm, risks being deemed an unenforceable restraint of trade. The five-year period is more likely to be viewed as excessive in restricting the seller's future employment opportunities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: BDO Seidman v. Hirshberg :: 1999 :: New York Court of Appeals Decisions :: New York Case Law :: New York Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia BDO Seidman v. Hirshberg Annotate this Case 93 N.Y.2d 382 (1999) 712 N.E.2d 1220 690 N.Y.S.2d 854 BDO SEIDMAN, Appellant, v. JEFFREY HIRSHBERG, Respondent. Court of Appeals of the State of New York. Argued March 24, 1999. Decided May 13, 1999. *385 Willkie Farr & Gallagher, New York City (Michael R. Young and Jeffrey O. Grossman of counsel), Kavinoky & Cook, L. L. P., Buffalo (Joseph J. Welter of counsel), Scott M. Univer and Barbara A. Taylor, of the Illinois Bar, admitted pro hac vice, for appellant. Jaeckle Fleischmann & Mugel, L. L. P., Buffalo (Philip H. McIntyre and Jennifer M. Demert of counsel), for respondent. *386 Vedder, Price, Kaufman, Kammholz & Day, New York City (Dan L. Goldwasser and Michael J. Crisafulli of counsel), for New York State Society of Certified Public Accountants, amicus curiae. Chief Judge KAYE and Judges BELLACOSA, SMITH, CIPARICK, WESLEY and ROSENBLATT concur. *387 OPINION OF THE COURT LEVINE, J. BDO Seidman (BDO), a general partnership of certified public accountants, appeals from the affirmance of an order of Supreme Court granting summary judgment dismissing its complaint against defendant, who was formerly employed as an accountant with the firm. The central issue before us is whether the "reimbursement clause" in an agreement between the parties, requiring defendant to compensate BDO for serving any client of the firm's Buffalo office within 18 months after the termination of his employment, is an invalid and unenforceable restrictive covenant. The courts below so held. Facts and Procedural History BDO is a national accounting firm having 40 offices throughout the United States, including four in New York State. Defendant began employment in BDO's Buffalo office in 1984, when the accounting firm he had been working for was merged into BDO, its partners becoming BDO partners. In 1989, defendant was promoted to the position of manager
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period from three to five years. The Law Explanation indicates that New York law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in time and scope. The Law Explanation also states that a five-year period, especially without a narrowly defined geographic area, risks being deemed an unenforceable restraint of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses a case, BDO Seidman v. Hirshberg, concerning restrictive covenants. Although the specifics of that case differ, it supports the general principle that New York courts scrutinize non-compete agreements for reasonableness. The perturbation explanation also states that extending the non-compete period to five years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under New York law. Based on this information, the modification likely creates a legal contradiction because it increases the risk of the non-compete agreement being deemed unenforceable under New York law.

perturbation.original_text: Seller agrees that for a period of one (1) year after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing
perturbation.changed_text: Seller agrees that for a period of five (5) years after the execution of this Agreement, it will not in any way, directly or indirectly, for itself or on behalf of or in conjunction with any other person, partnership, firm or corporation hire any employee of the Division or Purchasers as of the Closing
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under New York law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding the reasonableness of non-solicitation agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: Ticor Title Ins. Co. v. Cohen, 173 F.3d 63, 70 (2d Cir. 1999)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York courts disfavor non-solicitation agreements that are broader than necessary to protect an employer's legitimate business interests. While a one-year non-solicitation agreement is more likely to be upheld, a five-year agreement is presumptively unreasonable unless the employer can demonstrate a specific need for such a long period, such as the protection of unique client relationships or trade secrets. This is not directly related to trade secrets, making this extension problematic.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a one-year non-solicitation period, while the changed text extends this period to five years. According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, New York courts may view a five-year non-solicitation agreement as an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly if the employer cannot demonstrate a specific need for such an extended period. While the provided Scraped Law Snippet 1 is from GovInfo and thus doesn't directly contain the specific legal text, the overall context suggests a legal concern that a longer period is potentially unenforceable, meaning the modified text creates a legal contradiction according to the definition given for 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: The terms of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California introduces potential conflicts if the agreement contains provisions that are valid under New York law but not under California law, or vice versa.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California's stricter approach to non-compete agreements under Business and Professions Code Section 16600.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements, with limited exceptions (such as the sale of a business). If the agreement contains non-compete provisions that are broader than what is permitted under California law, changing the governing law to California would render those provisions unenforceable, creating a contradiction if the parties intended New York law to apply to those provisions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from New York to California, creating a potential contradiction regarding the enforceability of non-compete agreements. According to the provided Law Explanation, California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements, with limited exceptions. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600 (a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," except as provided in the chapter. If the original agreement contains non-compete provisions that are valid under New York law but not under California law, changing the governing law to California would render those provisions unenforceable. This directly contradicts the parties' potential intent for New York law to apply to those specific provisions, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. Therefore, the modification introduces a legal contradiction.
